findings.title: Missing standard allowed-tools declaration in manifest
findings.description: The skill does not declare the standard `allowed-tools` field in the manifest, even though auxiliary metadata suggests tool usage (`allowed_tools`: Bash, Read, Write). This reduces transparency for tool governance and makes it harder to validate whether the skill's behaviors align with declared permissions. Based on the provided content, this is informational rather than an active violation because no explicit standard restriction is declared and no standalone script files are present.
findings.snippet:
allowed-tools: Not specified
additional metadata: {'allowed_tools': ['Bash', 'Read', 'Write'], 'network': True}
findings.remediation: Add a proper `allowed-tools` field using the expected schema and include all required tools explicitly. If networked AWS CLI usage is intended, document that clearly in the manifest and instructions.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The manifest content provided does omit a standard `allowed-tools` declaration, and the finding itself already frames this as informational transparency rather than active abuse.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Low; this is not directly exploitable and only affects governance visibility.
metadata.meta_impact: Reduced transparency about expected tool usage, which may complicate policy review but does not by itself enable unauthorized actions.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill appears to implement a straightforward S3 backup workflow using AWS CLI commands and environment-based configuration. No direct prompt injection, credential theft, hardcoded secrets, obfuscated payloads, or explicit command-injection patterns were identified in the provided content. The main issue is weak manifest hygiene: missing standard metadata for allowed tools and compatibility reduces auditability. The backup behavior does transmit local project data to Amazon S3 by design, but this is consistent with the stated purpose rather than covert exfiltration.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings concern missing manifest metadata fields that affect transparency/documentation rather than runtime security behavior.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add a proper YAML front matter manifest to SKILL.md including explicit allowed-tools, license, compatibility, and any other required metadata fields.

meta_recommendations.title: Add explicit manifest front matter
meta_recommendations.fix:
Prepend SKILL.md with YAML front matter such as: `---
name: s3-backup
description: Back up project files to Amazon S3
license: MIT
compatibility: [Claude.ai, Claude Code]
allowed-tools: [Bash, Read, Write]
---`.

meta_recommendations.title: Clarify that the Python block is illustrative and uses a fixed command
meta_recommendations.fix: Add a note above the Python example stating that it runs a fixed AWS CLI verification command only, does not invoke a shell, and should remain free of user-supplied arguments; optionally replace the snippet with `check=True` and an explanatory comment.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill is a benign backup guide that uses standard AWS CLI commands; the reported issues are informational or false positives rather than security threats.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Add explicit manifest metadata such as allowed-tools and license for transparency and governance.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The skill content shows ordinary backup and restore instructions plus a simple credential-validation helper that runs a fixed AWS CLI command without user-controlled input, exfiltration, obfuscation, or deceptive behavior.
